catalyst.source: The deposition of the Ni(OH) 2 on the as-prepared C 3 N 4 samples was carried out using the conventional precipitation method (Yu et al. 2013).
catalyst.context: Preparation of the solid silica (SiO 2 ) template The SiO 2 templates were produced according to the previously published Stober method with minor modification (Qin et al. 2014).In detail, 12 mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was quickly put into a blend of ethanol (148 mL), deionized water (20 mL), and aqueous ammonium solution (25-28, 6.30 mL).The blended solution was then mixed for 1 h under room temperature, ensuring the production of a white silica suspension.The resultant silica particles were detached from the solution and washed several times with ethanol and water.The as-produced 100 mg of SiO 2 nanosphere was dissipated in 20 mL of water and ultrasonicated for 15 min.The colloidal SiO 2 nanospheres were later dispersed into a solution containing water (60 mL), ethanol (30 mL), and ammonia solution (0.55 mL) with cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) (150 mg).After the suspension was rotated for 0.5 h, 0.25 mL of TEOS was rapidly mixed, and later after 6 h, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and water and dried in an oven at 80 C for 6 h.The asprepared product was calcinated at 550 C in the air at a heating speed of 2 C min -1 for 6 h to eliminate CTAB to form the SiO 2 template. To prepare HC-C 3 N 4 , the as-prepared SiO 2 template was suspended in 60 mL of distilled water containing 10 g of dicyandiamide, mixed for overnight and then sonicated at 30 C for 2 h.Subsequently, the suspension was stirred at 60 C for the water to evaporate, and the resultant white solid was replaced into an alumina crucible and heated to 550 C under N 2 gas for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 C min -1 .The attained g-C 3 N 4 SiO 2 product was soaked with 4 M NH 4 HF 2 for 24 h to eliminate silica template.The resultant product was then centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water and ethanol.The obtained HC-C 3 N 4 product was later dried in an oven at 80 C for 12 h. The bulk g-C 3 N 4 was prepared by heating of dicyandiamide (2.0 g) from ambient temperature to 550 C under an N 2 atmosphere at a ramping rate 5 C min -1, kept for 4 h in a furnace, and then cooled naturally to ambient temperature and well ground in an agate mortar to obtain a bulk g-C 3 N 4 powder. The deposition of the Ni(OH) 2 on the as-prepared C 3 N 4 samples was carried out using the conventional precipitation method (Yu et al. 2013).In detail, 0.2 g of the as-prepared HC-C 3 N 4 sample was discharged in 0.5 M NaOH solution (50 mL).Then, a known volume of 0.05 M Ni(NO 3 ) 2 aqueous solution was mixed.The resultant blended solution was further stirred for 6 h at room temperature.Finally, the suspension was collected by centrifuge and washed several times with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 80 C for 24 h.The molar ratio (R) of Ni(OH) 2 to g-C 3 N 4 was fixed at 1.5. The chemical composition characterization was tested using Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu K irradiation (Rigaku, Japan).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was recorded using a Tecnai G 2 20 U-Twin electron microscope.The element mapping and field emission scanning electron microscopy were performed using the FESEM Hitachi S-4800 instrument, connected with energydispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford Instruments, Britain).The thermal stability of the as-prepared samples was recorded with a thermogravimetric analyzer (DTA-60, Shimadzu, Japan), from room temperature to 900 C under an ambient atmosphere at a ramping rate of 10 C min -1 .The BET surface areas of the as-prepared samples were recorded using Micromeritics ASAP 2020 (USA) (Sing 1985).The XPS measurement was performed using Mg K at 1253.6 eV radiation (VGESCALAB 210).The optical property of the samples was recorded using a UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan).The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were evaluated u si n g ( IR A ff i n i t y-1 F T-I R s pe c t r om et er ).T h e photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).The time-resolved transient PL decay curves were recorded with an FLS920 fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) under the excitation of 325 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction experiments were conducted using a 200 mL Pyrex reactor at ambient temperature and pressure condition (Wang et al. 2015b).The CO 2 reduction experiments are similar to our previously reported method (Mao et al. 2013a).A 300 W Xe arc lamp with UV cut-off filter ( 400 nm) was employed as an excitation light source and wavelength.The CO 2 reduction yield was quantified using a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu GC-2014C, Japan) operational with a flame-ionized locator and methanizer. The photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out in a typical three-electrode, cubicle quartz cell on an electrochemical work station (CH1660C instruments, CHI.China) similar to the previous publications (Yu et al. 2014a, b).The Mott-Schottky plots were measured using 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 solution at ambient temperature.The potential range was -1.0 to 2.0 V and at different frequencies of 1, 2 ,and 3 kHz with an AC voltage magnitude of 10 mV and a scan rate of 10 mV s -1 .

catalyst.source: Honeycomb-like graphitic carbon nitride (HC-C 3 N 4 ) was fabricated through thermal compression of an as-prepared SiO 2 template and dicyandiamide.
catalyst.context: Honeycomb-like graphitic carbon nitride (HC-C 3 N 4 ) was fabricated through thermal compression of an as-prepared SiO 2 template and dicyandiamide.The NH 4 HF 2 etching out of the SiO 2 template results in the creation of hollows or macropores in the C 3 N 4 matrix leading to the superior specific surface area.The HC-C 3 N 4 shows only structural changes but not in chemical composition correlated to the bulk C 3 N 4 .Compared with the bulk C 3 N 4 , the HC-C 3 N 4 nanostructure demonstrated a higher photocatalytic CO 2 reduction rate of hydrocarbon formation.The higher photocatalytic activity showed by HC-C 3 N 4 could be credited to the void nature of the structure, higher surface area, excellent visible light absorption ability, higher electron donor density, mass diffusion of materials for surface redox reaction, and effective segregation of photogenerated charge carriers due to the decrease in its wall thickness.Further, to confirm the enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity, Ni(OH) 2 nanoparticles were grown over the honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 structure to provide electron sink and to encourage charge dissolution efficiency.The asprepared Ni(OH) 2 decorated honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 hybrids displayed an excellent visible light photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to solar fuels with 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation, respectively.This study showed that the morphology tuning of C 3 N 4 can be an excellent strategy of producing low-cost and sustainable photocatalyst for mitigating increase CO 2 levels in the environment into clean solar fuels. Fig. 2 FESEM (a-d) and TEM (e-f) images of HC-C 3 N 4 Fig.6Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size allocation curves (inset) of as-prepared samples Fig. 7 a XPS survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of b C 1s and c N 1s of HC-C 3 N 4 and bulk C 3 N 4 samples Fig. 10 Time-resolved transient fluorescence decay curves of bulk C 3 N 4 and HC-C 3 N 4 samples excited at the wavelength of 325 nm Fig. 12 a Mott-Schottky plots carried out at various frequencies.b Scheme of band energy level structures Fig. 13 a Chromatogram of HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst after 1 h visible light irradiation.b Hydrocarbon production rate over as-prepared samples under visible light ( 400 nm) irradiation for 1 h Fig. 14 a Cycling runs of CO 2 reduction activity over HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst (1) CH 4 and (2) CH 3 OH production, b Powder X-ray diffraction of HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst before and after CO 2 reduction activity Fig. 15 Transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples in 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under visible light irradiation Physical properties the as-prepared samples Comparison of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction over previously reported C 3 N 4 -based photocatalysts with those synthesized in this work Funding information This work was supported by the Ghana Government Book and Research Allowance for tertiary institutions and also the Ton Duc Thang University, Vietnam for funding. The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest. Publisher's note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

catalyst.source: Fig. 13 a Chromatogram of HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst after 1 h visible light irradiation.
catalyst.context: Honeycomb-like graphitic carbon nitride (HC-C 3 N 4 ) was fabricated through thermal compression of an as-prepared SiO 2 template and dicyandiamide.The NH 4 HF 2 etching out of the SiO 2 template results in the creation of hollows or macropores in the C 3 N 4 matrix leading to the superior specific surface area.The HC-C 3 N 4 shows only structural changes but not in chemical composition correlated to the bulk C 3 N 4 .Compared with the bulk C 3 N 4 , the HC-C 3 N 4 nanostructure demonstrated a higher photocatalytic CO 2 reduction rate of hydrocarbon formation.The higher photocatalytic activity showed by HC-C 3 N 4 could be credited to the void nature of the structure, higher surface area, excellent visible light absorption ability, higher electron donor density, mass diffusion of materials for surface redox reaction, and effective segregation of photogenerated charge carriers due to the decrease in its wall thickness.Further, to confirm the enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity, Ni(OH) 2 nanoparticles were grown over the honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 structure to provide electron sink and to encourage charge dissolution efficiency.The asprepared Ni(OH) 2 decorated honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 hybrids displayed an excellent visible light photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to solar fuels with 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation, respectively.This study showed that the morphology tuning of C 3 N 4 can be an excellent strategy of producing low-cost and sustainable photocatalyst for mitigating increase CO 2 levels in the environment into clean solar fuels. Fig. 2 FESEM (a-d) and TEM (e-f) images of HC-C 3 N 4 Fig.6Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size allocation curves (inset) of as-prepared samples Fig. 7 a XPS survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of b C 1s and c N 1s of HC-C 3 N 4 and bulk C 3 N 4 samples Fig. 10 Time-resolved transient fluorescence decay curves of bulk C 3 N 4 and HC-C 3 N 4 samples excited at the wavelength of 325 nm Fig. 12 a Mott-Schottky plots carried out at various frequencies.b Scheme of band energy level structures Fig. 13 a Chromatogram of HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst after 1 h visible light irradiation.b Hydrocarbon production rate over as-prepared samples under visible light ( 400 nm) irradiation for 1 h Fig. 14 a Cycling runs of CO 2 reduction activity over HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst (1) CH 4 and (2) CH 3 OH production, b Powder X-ray diffraction of HC-C 3 N 4 Ni(OH) 2 photocatalyst before and after CO 2 reduction activity Fig. 15 Transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples in 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under visible light irradiation Physical properties the as-prepared samples Comparison of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction over previously reported C 3 N 4 -based photocatalysts with those synthesized in this work Funding information This work was supported by the Ghana Government Book and Research Allowance for tertiary institutions and also the Ton Duc Thang University, Vietnam for funding. The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest. Publisher's note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

co-catalyst.source: Furthermore, the HC-C 3 N 4 honeycomb structure was deposited with Ni(OH) 2 clusters which showed remarkable CO 2 reduction activity of 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation which is 3.5 and 4.3 times higher CO 2 reduction activity compared with bulk C 3 N 4 clustered with Ni(OH) 2 particles.
co-catalyst.context: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ) is paying attention lately owing to its interesting characteristics and substantial application in improving environmental and energy concerns.Nevertheless, the photocatalytic activity of g-C 3 N 4 is constrained by the inertness of the surface and particle aggregation during photocatalytic activity.Herein, we report the preparation of g-C 3 N 4 with honeycomb-like morphology (HC-C 3 N 4 ) via thermal condensation of prepared SiO 2 templates and dicyandiamide.The etching out of the SiO 2 templates by NH 4 HF 2 created hollow or macropores in the C 3 N 4 matrix resulting in its structural changes.Similar, to the bulk C 3 N 4 , the HC-C 3 N 4 exhibited higher photocatalytic CO 2 reduction in hydrocarbons.This improved photocatalytic achievement is associated with higher specific surface area, excellent visible light absorption capability, higher electron donor density, easy mass diffusion of materials for surface reaction, and effective segregation of photogenerated charge carriers.Furthermore, the HC-C 3 N 4 honeycomb structure was deposited with Ni(OH) 2 clusters which showed remarkable CO 2 reduction activity of 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation which is 3.5 and 4.3 times higher CO 2 reduction activity compared with bulk C 3 N 4 clustered with Ni(OH) 2 particles.This comprehensive study demonstrated that HC-C 3 N 4 nanostructured polymeric semiconductor is envisaged to have great potential in the application of a variety of fields such as photocatalysis, sensor technology, and nanotechnology.

co-catalyst.source: The deposition of the Ni(OH) 2 on the as-prepared C 3 N 4 samples was carried out using the conventional precipitation method (Yu et al. 2013).
co-catalyst.context: Preparation of the solid silica (SiO 2 ) template The SiO 2 templates were produced according to the previously published Stober method with minor modification (Qin et al. 2014).In detail, 12 mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was quickly put into a blend of ethanol (148 mL), deionized water (20 mL), and aqueous ammonium solution (25-28, 6.30 mL).The blended solution was then mixed for 1 h under room temperature, ensuring the production of a white silica suspension.The resultant silica particles were detached from the solution and washed several times with ethanol and water.The as-produced 100 mg of SiO 2 nanosphere was dissipated in 20 mL of water and ultrasonicated for 15 min.The colloidal SiO 2 nanospheres were later dispersed into a solution containing water (60 mL), ethanol (30 mL), and ammonia solution (0.55 mL) with cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) (150 mg).After the suspension was rotated for 0.5 h, 0.25 mL of TEOS was rapidly mixed, and later after 6 h, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and water and dried in an oven at 80 C for 6 h.The asprepared product was calcinated at 550 C in the air at a heating speed of 2 C min -1 for 6 h to eliminate CTAB to form the SiO 2 template. To prepare HC-C 3 N 4 , the as-prepared SiO 2 template was suspended in 60 mL of distilled water containing 10 g of dicyandiamide, mixed for overnight and then sonicated at 30 C for 2 h.Subsequently, the suspension was stirred at 60 C for the water to evaporate, and the resultant white solid was replaced into an alumina crucible and heated to 550 C under N 2 gas for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 C min -1 .The attained g-C 3 N 4 SiO 2 product was soaked with 4 M NH 4 HF 2 for 24 h to eliminate silica template.The resultant product was then centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water and ethanol.The obtained HC-C 3 N 4 product was later dried in an oven at 80 C for 12 h. The bulk g-C 3 N 4 was prepared by heating of dicyandiamide (2.0 g) from ambient temperature to 550 C under an N 2 atmosphere at a ramping rate 5 C min -1, kept for 4 h in a furnace, and then cooled naturally to ambient temperature and well ground in an agate mortar to obtain a bulk g-C 3 N 4 powder. The deposition of the Ni(OH) 2 on the as-prepared C 3 N 4 samples was carried out using the conventional precipitation method (Yu et al. 2013).In detail, 0.2 g of the as-prepared HC-C 3 N 4 sample was discharged in 0.5 M NaOH solution (50 mL).Then, a known volume of 0.05 M Ni(NO 3 ) 2 aqueous solution was mixed.The resultant blended solution was further stirred for 6 h at room temperature.Finally, the suspension was collected by centrifuge and washed several times with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 80 C for 24 h.The molar ratio (R) of Ni(OH) 2 to g-C 3 N 4 was fixed at 1.5. The chemical composition characterization was tested using Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu K irradiation (Rigaku, Japan).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was recorded using a Tecnai G 2 20 U-Twin electron microscope.The element mapping and field emission scanning electron microscopy were performed using the FESEM Hitachi S-4800 instrument, connected with energydispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford Instruments, Britain).The thermal stability of the as-prepared samples was recorded with a thermogravimetric analyzer (DTA-60, Shimadzu, Japan), from room temperature to 900 C under an ambient atmosphere at a ramping rate of 10 C min -1 .The BET surface areas of the as-prepared samples were recorded using Micromeritics ASAP 2020 (USA) (Sing 1985).The XPS measurement was performed using Mg K at 1253.6 eV radiation (VGESCALAB 210).The optical property of the samples was recorded using a UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan).The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were evaluated u si n g ( IR A ff i n i t y-1 F T-I R s pe c t r om et er ).T h e photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).The time-resolved transient PL decay curves were recorded with an FLS920 fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) under the excitation of 325 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction experiments were conducted using a 200 mL Pyrex reactor at ambient temperature and pressure condition (Wang et al. 2015b).The CO 2 reduction experiments are similar to our previously reported method (Mao et al. 2013a).A 300 W Xe arc lamp with UV cut-off filter ( 400 nm) was employed as an excitation light source and wavelength.The CO 2 reduction yield was quantified using a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu GC-2014C, Japan) operational with a flame-ionized locator and methanizer. The photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out in a typical three-electrode, cubicle quartz cell on an electrochemical work station (CH1660C instruments, CHI.China) similar to the previous publications (Yu et al. 2014a, b).The Mott-Schottky plots were measured using 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 solution at ambient temperature.The potential range was -1.0 to 2.0 V and at different frequencies of 1, 2 ,and 3 kHz with an AC voltage magnitude of 10 mV and a scan rate of 10 mV s -1 .

co-catalyst.source: Further, to confirm the enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity, Ni(OH) 2 nanoparticles were grown over the honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 structure to provide electron sink and to encourage charge dissolution efficiency.
co-catalyst.context: Honeycomb-like graphitic carbon nitride (HC-C 3 N 4 ) was fabricated through thermal compression of an as-prepared SiO 2 template and dicyandiamide.The NH 4 HF 2 etching out of the SiO 2 template results in the creation of hollows or macropores in the C 3 N 4 matrix leading to the superior specific surface area.The HC-C 3 N 4 shows only structural changes but not in chemical composition correlated to the bulk C 3 N 4 .Compared with the bulk C 3 N 4 , the HC-C 3 N 4 nanostructure demonstrated a higher photocatalytic CO 2 reduction rate of hydrocarbon formation.The higher photocatalytic activity showed by HC-C 3 N 4 could be credited to the void nature of the structure, higher surface area, excellent visible light absorption ability, higher electron donor density, mass diffusion of materials for surface redox reaction, and effective segregation of photogenerated charge carriers due to the decrease in its wall thickness.Further, to confirm the enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity, Ni(OH) 2 nanoparticles were grown over the honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 structure to provide electron sink and to encourage charge dissolution efficiency.The asprepared Ni(OH) 2 decorated honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 hybrids displayed an excellent visible light photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to solar fuels with 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation, respectively.This study showed that the morphology tuning of C 3 N 4 can be an excellent strategy of producing low-cost and sustainable photocatalyst for mitigating increase CO 2 levels in the environment into clean solar fuels. lamp.source: A 300 W Xe arc lamp with UV cut-off filter ( 400 nm) was employed as an excitation light source and wavelength.
lamp.context: Preparation of the solid silica (SiO 2 ) template The SiO 2 templates were produced according to the previously published Stober method with minor modification (Qin et al. 2014).In detail, 12 mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was quickly put into a blend of ethanol (148 mL), deionized water (20 mL), and aqueous ammonium solution (25-28, 6.30 mL).The blended solution was then mixed for 1 h under room temperature, ensuring the production of a white silica suspension.The resultant silica particles were detached from the solution and washed several times with ethanol and water.The as-produced 100 mg of SiO 2 nanosphere was dissipated in 20 mL of water and ultrasonicated for 15 min.The colloidal SiO 2 nanospheres were later dispersed into a solution containing water (60 mL), ethanol (30 mL), and ammonia solution (0.55 mL) with cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) (150 mg).After the suspension was rotated for 0.5 h, 0.25 mL of TEOS was rapidly mixed, and later after 6 h, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and water and dried in an oven at 80 C for 6 h.The asprepared product was calcinated at 550 C in the air at a heating speed of 2 C min -1 for 6 h to eliminate CTAB to form the SiO 2 template. To prepare HC-C 3 N 4 , the as-prepared SiO 2 template was suspended in 60 mL of distilled water containing 10 g of dicyandiamide, mixed for overnight and then sonicated at 30 C for 2 h.Subsequently, the suspension was stirred at 60 C for the water to evaporate, and the resultant white solid was replaced into an alumina crucible and heated to 550 C under N 2 gas for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 C min -1 .The attained g-C 3 N 4 SiO 2 product was soaked with 4 M NH 4 HF 2 for 24 h to eliminate silica template.The resultant product was then centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water and ethanol.The obtained HC-C 3 N 4 product was later dried in an oven at 80 C for 12 h. The bulk g-C 3 N 4 was prepared by heating of dicyandiamide (2.0 g) from ambient temperature to 550 C under an N 2 atmosphere at a ramping rate 5 C min -1, kept for 4 h in a furnace, and then cooled naturally to ambient temperature and well ground in an agate mortar to obtain a bulk g-C 3 N 4 powder. The deposition of the Ni(OH) 2 on the as-prepared C 3 N 4 samples was carried out using the conventional precipitation method (Yu et al. 2013).In detail, 0.2 g of the as-prepared HC-C 3 N 4 sample was discharged in 0.5 M NaOH solution (50 mL).Then, a known volume of 0.05 M Ni(NO 3 ) 2 aqueous solution was mixed.The resultant blended solution was further stirred for 6 h at room temperature.Finally, the suspension was collected by centrifuge and washed several times with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 80 C for 24 h.The molar ratio (R) of Ni(OH) 2 to g-C 3 N 4 was fixed at 1.5. The chemical composition characterization was tested using Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu K irradiation (Rigaku, Japan).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was recorded using a Tecnai G 2 20 U-Twin electron microscope.The element mapping and field emission scanning electron microscopy were performed using the FESEM Hitachi S-4800 instrument, connected with energydispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford Instruments, Britain).The thermal stability of the as-prepared samples was recorded with a thermogravimetric analyzer (DTA-60, Shimadzu, Japan), from room temperature to 900 C under an ambient atmosphere at a ramping rate of 10 C min -1 .The BET surface areas of the as-prepared samples were recorded using Micromeritics ASAP 2020 (USA) (Sing 1985).The XPS measurement was performed using Mg K at 1253.6 eV radiation (VGESCALAB 210).The optical property of the samples was recorded using a UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan).The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were evaluated u si n g ( IR A ff i n i t y-1 F T-I R s pe c t r om et er ).T h e photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).The time-resolved transient PL decay curves were recorded with an FLS920 fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) under the excitation of 325 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction experiments were conducted using a 200 mL Pyrex reactor at ambient temperature and pressure condition (Wang et al. 2015b).The CO 2 reduction experiments are similar to our previously reported method (Mao et al. 2013a).A 300 W Xe arc lamp with UV cut-off filter ( 400 nm) was employed as an excitation light source and wavelength.The CO 2 reduction yield was quantified using a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu GC-2014C, Japan) operational with a flame-ionized locator and methanizer. The photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out in a typical three-electrode, cubicle quartz cell on an electrochemical work station (CH1660C instruments, CHI.China) similar to the previous publications (Yu et al. 2014a, b).The Mott-Schottky plots were measured using 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 solution at ambient temperature.The potential range was -1.0 to 2.0 V and at different frequencies of 1, 2 ,and 3 kHz with an AC voltage magnitude of 10 mV and a scan rate of 10 mV s -1 .

reaction medium.source: Fig. 15 Transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples in 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under visible light irradiation
reaction medium.context: Honeycomb-like graphitic carbon nitride (HC-C 3 N 4 ) was fabricated through thermal compression of an as-prepared SiO 2 template and dicyandiamide.The NH 4 HF 2 etching out of the SiO 2 template results in the creation of hollows or macropores in the C 3 N 4 matrix leading to the superior specific surface area.The HC-C 3 N 4 shows only structural changes but not in chemical composition correlated to the bulk C 3 N 4 .Compared with the bulk C 3 N 4 , the HC-C 3 N 4 nanostructure demonstrated a higher photocatalytic CO 2 reduction rate of hydrocarbon formation.The higher photocatalytic activity showed by HC-C 3 N 4 could be credited to the void nature of the structure, higher surface area, excellent visible light absorption ability, higher electron donor density, mass diffusion of materials for surface redox reaction, and effective segregation of photogenerated charge carriers due to the decrease in its wall thickness.Further, to confirm the enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity, Ni(OH) 2 nanoparticles were grown over the honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 structure to provide electron sink and to encourage charge dissolution efficiency.The asprepared Ni(OH) 2 decorated honeycomb g-C 3 N 4 hybrids displayed an excellent visible light photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to solar fuels with 1.48 molh -1 g -1 of CH 4 and 0.73 molh -1 g -1 of CH 3 OH generation, respectively.This study showed that the morphology tuning of C 3 N 4 can be an excellent strategy of producing low-cost and sustainable photocatalyst for mitigating increase CO 2 levels in the environment into clean solar fuels.
